column4: Does the prompt reflect the assessed skill?
column6: Is there a single correct answer?
column7: Is there parallelism among the alternatives?
column10: Do the alternatives include elements not present in the stimulus?
column11: Is the item adequately aligned with the assessed skill?
column12: Could the item be used in an assessment with little or no modification?
column13: Observation
The alternatives are not plausible in terms of length.
Although plausibility is better in this item compared to the previous one, the answer key stands out due to its length.
This is the best-constructed item: the AI trims the answer key, meeting the plausibility criterion (length), and selects excerpts with only one conjunction.

In this item, the AI begins the alternatives with logical-discursive relation markers, with the answer key being longer. Thus, the issue of parallelism among the alternatives remains, which compromises plausibility.
Same issue of parallelism. The logical-discursive marker in alternative C was created by the AI.
Alternatives A and D were modified by the AI. Alternative A loses parallelism.

Here, the AI alters the answer key, transforming the conditional relation into a negative. This further compromises plausibility compared to previous constructions, making the answer key even more attractive than when a cohesive link was inserted.
Here, the AI also alters the structure of the answer key, working with the idea in the text regarding the condition for the girl's qualification in the tournament.
Best construction by the AI: aligned with the template.
The answer key is incorrect: “when” can be a conditional marker, but not in this example. Item discarded.
The news about the picnic does not present a source. Best AI construction: the item meets the skill, but still has the parallelism issue (length).
The AI adds a time marker (“During the picnic”) to all alternatives and adapts them using elements from the text.
The AI also modifies the alternatives by incorporating elements from the text and adding cohesive links that are not present in the original text.

The AI modifies elements of the alternatives but keeps the answer key intact. Alternative A changes verb tense; alternative B modifies the ending (summarizes); alternative C omits a segment. These changes result in good syntactic parallelism with balanced length.
Construction identical to item 23, with variation in the order of alternatives.

The alternatives are not plausible, as the connector “and” used in two of the four alternatives indicates addition of elements rather than clauses. Additionally, the third distractor (D) contains two connectors. Only the answer key aligns with the template description, but it includes a cut from the text that was not indicated by the AI.

The alternatives are plausible in terms of length, but alternative C does not include a conjunction as claimed in the justification. The answer key is the same as in the previous item, now fully restored.
Same structure as previous items with the same text; one alternative is not plausible due to the absence of a conjunction.
Same structure as previous items with the same text; one alternative is not plausible due to the absence of a conjunction. The AI rearranges the order, changing the position of the answer key.

Same as previous comments. None of the items produced by the AI with this text (items 26–30) would be recommended, because it is not possible to produce four alternatives while maintaining the template requirement that all express different logical-discursive relations.
The answer key corresponds to a modified excerpt of the text, which differentiates it from the other alternatives. There is no parallelism among the alternatives—they differ significantly in length.
Lack of parallelism — length of alternatives.

The answer options are paraphrases of excerpts from the text, and the command asks for recognition of a text excerpt expressing a conditional idea. Therefore, the alternatives do not correspond to what the command requires.

The answer key is a paraphrase of a statement from the text and not an excerpt taken directly from it. It has also already been used in previous items. There is no parallelism among the alternatives (length).
Distractor C corresponds to a modified excerpt of the text, conflicting with the command and even with the model item. There is no parallelism among the alternatives (length).

The answer key is incorrect because the conjunction “sem que” does not express condition. The alternatives significantly modify the text excerpts, weakening their relation to the command and contradicting the item template.

The answer key is incorrect because the conjunction “sem que” does not express condition. The alternatives are not excerpts from the text, contradicting the template guidelines. They are paraphrases. There is no parallelism among the alternatives.

The answer key modifies the text excerpt by replacing the temporal conjunction “after” with the conditional “if,” thereby turning the clause into a conditional. The alternatives are not excerpts from the text, and there is no parallelism among them.

The answer key modifies the text excerpt by replacing the temporal conjunction “after” with the conditional “if,” thereby turning the clause into a conditional. Additionally, alternatives A and D are, as in other cases, paraphrases of the same excerpt from the text.

The item has no valid answer: the selected answer involves an incorrect analysis of the word “se,” which is clearly not a conditional conjunction. The alternatives are not excerpts from the text. There is no parallelism among the alternatives (length).
Lack of parallelism: alternative B (the answer key) is much longer than the others, making it stand out.

The item cannot be used because the answer alternatives are not excerpts from the text, which contradicts both the command and the template guidelines. Additionally, item 52 is practically identical to the previous one. Justification A is incorrect, as “que” is not an explanatory conjunction but a relative pronoun.
